title: The practice of making choices based on what you want not what you should
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

The day I realized I didn't know what I wanted

htmlContent:
The day I realized I didn't know what I wanted

I sat in my car for twenty minutes. Engine off. Parking lot of a grocery store I didn't need to be at. I was trying to decide if I should go inside and buy the organic kale or just drive home. And I couldn't do it. I literally could not make that decision. Not because I was tired or hungry or stressed. Because I had no idea what I actually wanted.

Here's the thing. I had spent thirty years making decisions based on what I should do. What a good partner should do. What a responsible adult should do. What a trauma survivor who is "healing correctly" should do. And somewhere along the way, the part of me that knows what I want had gone completely silent. Not rebellious. Not defiant. Just... gone. Like a radio station that stopped broadcasting.

This is what happens when you grow up in environments where your wants were dangerous. Where wanting something meant risking rejection, punishment, or abandonment. Where you learned that safety lived in compliance, in anticipating others' needs, in becoming so small that no one noticed you had preferences at all.

I remember the first time a therapist asked me what I wanted for myself. Not what I thought I should want. Not what would make my parents proud. Not what would keep me safe. Just what I wanted. I stared at her for what felt like a full minute. My mind was completely blank. Like someone had erased a chalkboard. She waited. I started crying. Not sad tears. Confused tears. The kind that come when you realize you've been living someone else's life.

Wild, right? That we can be so disconnected from our own desires that the question "what do you want" feels like a foreign language. But it makes sense. When your nervous system learned early that your wants were a problem, it developed a brilliant survival strategy. It stopped generating them. Safer that way. No wants means no disappointment. No desire means no risk of rejection. Just a quiet, compliant existence that keeps you out of trouble.

But here's the cost. You don't get to feel alive. You don't get to feel your own energy moving through you. You become a ghost in your own body, going through motions that belong to someone else's script.

The nervous system's role in wanting

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, would tell you that our ability to know what we want is directly connected to our nervous system state. When we're in a state of safety, what he calls ventral vagal activation, we have access to our social engagement system. We can connect. We can play. We can explore. We can feel desire.

But when we're in a state of threat, either fight-or-flight or shutdown, that capacity collapses. Your brain prioritizes survival over preference. You don't get to ask "what do I want" when your nervous system thinks a tiger is in the room. You just react. You comply. You freeze. You do what kept you safe before.

This is why "just choose what you want" is such terrible advice for trauma survivors. It assumes the machinery for wanting is online and functioning. For many of us, it's not. It's been offline for years, maybe decades. The wiring got cut somewhere in childhood, and we've been running on autopilot ever since.

I started experimenting with small things. What color mug do I actually want to drink from? Not the one that matches the set. Not the one that was a gift. The one I want. I stood in my kitchen for five minutes. My hand kept reaching for the safe mug, the neutral one, the one no one would question. I made myself wait. I felt the anxiety rising. The voice in my head saying "it doesn't matter, just pick one, why are you making this complicated."

That voice. That's the voice of a nervous system trying to keep you safe. It's not trying to help you. It's trying to keep you invisible. Because visible wants are dangerous. Visible preferences are a target.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored by Deb Dana, which walks through exactly this process of learning to feel safe enough to access your own authentic responses. It's not about fixing yourself. It's about giving your nervous system new information that it's okay to want.

Stay with me here. This is not about becoming a selfish person. This is about reclaiming the capacity to know what you want so you can make choices that actually serve you. Because here's what I've learned. When you consistently choose based on what you should do, you end up resentful. Burned out. Disconnected. And eventually, your body will make the choice for you. It will get sick. It will collapse. It will force you to stop.

I've seen this in so many clients. The person who keeps saying yes to family obligations they hate. The one who stays in a job that drains them because it's "the right thing." The one who can't even order from a restaurant menu without asking what other people want first. These aren't character flaws. These are survival strategies that have outlived their usefulness.

The shoulds are not your friend

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma lives in the body as unfinished biological responses. The shoulds work the same way. They're unfinished commands from a past that no longer exists. "You should be grateful." "You should be nice." "You should put others first." These aren't truths. They're recordings. Old tapes playing in a room where no one is listening anymore.

But they feel true. Because they were true once. For a child who depended on caretakers for survival, following the shoulds was literally life-preserving. If you didn't do what you were supposed to do, you might be abandoned, punished, or hurt. Your brain learned that lesson deeply. It's not a belief. It's a body memory.

So when I say "choose what you want, not what you should," I'm not saying it's easy. I'm not saying it's a one-time decision. I'm saying it's a practice. A daily, sometimes hourly, practice of noticing the difference between what you actually want and what your survival brain is telling you is the safe choice.

I use a simple question now. When I feel the pull of a should, I ask myself: "If no one was watching, if no one would be disappointed, if there were no consequences at all, what would I choose?" And then I wait. Sometimes the answer comes immediately. Sometimes it takes days. Sometimes the answer is "I don't know" and that's okay too. The question itself is the practice.

A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is The Complex PTSD Workbook by Arielle Schwartz. It has specific exercises for identifying what you actually want versus what you've been conditioned to want. I found it helpful because it doesn't assume you already know. It walks you through the process of discovering it.

Think about it. When was the last time you made a choice based purely on what you wanted, without checking it against what you should do? Without running it through the filter of what others would think? Without justifying it to yourself first? If you can't remember, you're not alone. Most trauma survivors can't.

I started with food. What do I actually want to eat? Not what's healthy. Not what I should eat. Not what's on the meal plan. Just what sounds good. My body didn't know how to answer at first. It had been ignoring its own preferences for so long that the signals were faint. But I kept asking. And slowly, the signals got louder. I wanted eggs. I wanted a specific kind of bread. I wanted soup on cold days. Nothing dramatic. Just small preferences that were mine.

The fear of wanting

Here's what nobody tells you about choosing what you want. It's terrifying. Because once you admit you want something, you can fail to get it. You can be rejected. You can be disappointed. And for someone whose nervous system is wired for survival, disappointment feels like death. It's not. But it feels like it.

So the practice of choosing what you want is also the practice of learning to survive disappointment. Of learning that wanting and not getting won't kill you. Of learning that your worth isn't determined by whether your desires are fulfilled. This is slow work. There's no shortcut.

A practical guide to boundaries that many find accessible is Set Boundaries, Find Peace by Nedra Glover Tawwab. Because choosing what you want almost always requires boundaries. You have to say no to what you should do in order to say yes to what you want. And that means disappointing people. It means being seen as selfish. It means losing relationships that were built on your compliance.

I lost friends when I started choosing what I wanted. People who were used to me saying yes, showing up, accommodating. When I started saying "actually, I don't want to do that," some of them disappeared. And that hurt. But it also showed me which relationships were real and which were just transactions where I provided compliance in exchange for belonging.

Notice what just happened in your body. When I said that, did you feel a tightening somewhere? A resistance? A thought like "but I can't lose people" or "that's too risky"? That's the survival brain talking. It's not wrong. It's just protecting you from a threat that might not be there anymore.

I'm not saying go burn all your bridges. I'm saying start with one small choice. One moment where you choose what you want over what you should. One cup of coffee from the mug you actually like. One meal that isn't what you're supposed to eat. One conversation where you say "I don't want to do that" instead of "sure, no problem."

The return to yourself

This is what the category "the return" means on this site. Not a return to some idealized past. Not a return to who you were before trauma. A return to the part of you that knows what it wants. The part that was silenced, buried, or hidden for survival. The part that still exists, even if you can't hear it yet.

Janina Fisher, who writes about structural dissociation, talks about how different parts of us hold different experiences and responses. There's a part of you that learned to comply, to follow the shoulds, to stay small. That part kept you alive. Thank it. But it doesn't have to run your life anymore. There's another part that knows what you want. It might be quiet. It might be scared. But it's there.

A compassionate reframing of trauma that resonates with many is What Happened to You? by Bruce Perry and Oprah Winfrey. It helps shift the question from "what's wrong with me" to "what happened to me." And that shift is essential for this work. Because nothing is wrong with you for not knowing what you want. Something happened to you that made it unsafe to want. And now you get to learn that it's safe again.

I still struggle with this. I still catch myself making choices based on what I should do. I still feel the pull of old patterns, the comfort of compliance, the safety of invisibility. But now I notice it. And noticing it is the first step. The second step is asking the question. The third step is waiting for the answer, even if it takes a long time.

The practice of making choices based on what you want, not what you should, is not about getting everything you want. It's about reconnecting to your own desire. It's about feeling your own energy move through you. It's about being alive in your own life instead of just surviving someone else's.

I don't have a neat ending for this. There's no five-step plan that fixes everything. There's just the daily practice of asking yourself what you want and being brave enough to listen. Some days you'll hear the answer clearly. Some days you won't hear anything at all. Both are okay. Both are part of the return.

Sit with that.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Anchored by Deb Dana - A practical guide to working with your nervous system
  • The Complex PTSD Workbook by Arielle Schwartz - A structured workbook for C-PTSD survivors
  • Set Boundaries, Find Peace by Nedra Glover Tawwab - A practical guide to boundaries
  • What Happened to You? by Bruce Perry and Oprah Winfrey - A compassionate reframing of trauma
